{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

runContext.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
runContext.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI -- One-Shot Article Content Generation Spec (React Router TSX) -- v2026-04-26 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent (as a "system" / "developer" prompt) so the agent can generate brand-consistent MLAI article content modules in one shot for this repository. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Content should be practical, inclusive, and factual. Avoid hype and guarantees. Output format: TypeScript React component file (`.tsx`) for article body content. Framework: React Router 7 + React 19 + Tailwind. Import style: alias-first with `~/...` paths. --- ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given article inputs (slug/category/topic/body sections/FAQ/references/CTA text), generate a production-ready article content TSX file that compiles **without manual edits**. Framework constraints (required): - Use `react-router` APIs (e.g., `Link` with `to` prop). - Do **not** use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `use client`, metadata exports, etc.). - Do **not** use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use Tailwind utility classes consistent with this repo. Architecture constraints (required): - **NO `ArticleLayout` in content files**. Route handler wraps content already. - Content component must return `<>...` or `
...
`. - If using wrapper div, use `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Use design system components when available: - Persona cards → `AudienceGrid` (`variant`: `orange | purple | yellow` only) - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` (`variant`: `purple | orange` only) - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` (no raw `` blocks for article body sections) - Never use `placehold.co` URLs. Use `` or empty string placeholders. - Ensure `seo_config` path matches registry slug path exactly. Deliverable: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` (If requested, also provide companion updates for registry/SEO entries with matching slug/path.) --- ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: - `title`: string - `slug`: string - `category`: string (folder under `app/articles/content/`) - `summary`: string - `sections`: ordered list of body sections - `audienceCards` (optional): for "Who is this for?" - `quoteBlocks` (optional) - `images` (optional) - `faqItems` (optional) - `references` (optional) - `cta` (optional, generic wording only) Use placeholders exactly as provided (e.g., `{{TOPIC}}`). --- ## 2) Framework, file, and styling configuration - Language: TypeScript (`.tsx`) - Component pattern: `export default function ...() { ... }` - Routing library: `react-router` - Styling: Tailwind classes already used in repo - Typography plugin enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`) - Internal imports should prefer `~/...` aliases Common class patterns in this codebase: - Layout/spacing: `my-8`, `my-12`, `mt-8`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-3xl`, `p-6`, `sm:p-8` - Text colors: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600`, `text-white` - Borders/surfaces: `border border-gray-300`, `border-gray-400`, `bg-transparent` - Prose-safe wrappers for custom UI blocks: `not-prose` --- ## 3) Import rules (use real project paths) Use only imports that exist in this codebase. Typical imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Do not invent components/props. --- ## 4) Article content structure (no duplicate page header) Important: layout/hero/breadcrumb/title are handled upstream. So inside content file: - Start directly with body content (`h2`, `p`, lists, blocks). - Do **not** render duplicate title/breadcrumb/author header. - Keep heading hierarchy valid (`h2` for top-level sections, `h3` nested). Recommended section flow: 1. Intro paragraph(s) 2. Main sections (`h2`) 3. Optional persona block (`AudienceGrid`) 4. Optional quote block (`QuoteBlock`) 5. Optional image block (`ArticleImageBlock`) 6. FAQ (`ArticleFAQ`) 7. Generic CTA 8. Footer nav (`ArticleFooterNav`) --- ## 5) Design system component usage (strict) ### 5.1 Audience cards Use `AudienceGrid` for "Who is this for?" sections: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants only: `orange | purple | yellow`. ### 5.2 Quotes/callouts Use `QuoteBlock` (not ad-hoc bordered divs): ```tsx Keep your first AI implementation narrow, measurable, and reviewable. ``` Allowed variants only: `purple | orange`. ### 5.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`: ```tsx ``` No placeholder services (e.g., no `placehold.co`). --- ## 6) Links, references, and navigation - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` with `to`. - External links: `...`. - Use authoritative references when included. - Footer navigation can use: ```tsx ``` --- ## 7) CTA rules - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - ✅ Example: `Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}` - ❌ Do not use product-specific or service-specific names unless explicitly provided. Example with existing component: ```tsx ``` --- ## 8) Content quality and tone rules - Australian context where relevant. - Practical, factual, no guarantees. - No medical/legal-style warning blocks unless explicitly requested. - No service chips/tags sections. - Keep paragraphs concise and scannable. - Prefer concrete steps and examples. --- ## 9) Build-safety rules (one-shot accuracy) - File must compile with strict TypeScript. - Use valid, existing imports only. - Do not add unsupported props to components. - Keep variant strings within known unions. - Avoid fragile breadcrumb/icon patterns in content files. - Keep slug/path naming consistent across file path and SEO/registry references. --- ## 10) Brand QA checklist (must pass) - Uses repository design components for cards/callouts/images. - No duplicate hero/title section in content body. - Wrapper uses `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Tailwind classes align with existing style language. - CTA text remains generic with placeholders preserved. - No invented components. --- ## 11) Technical QA checklist (must pass) - React Router imports only (`react-router`). - No Next.js/Vue/Svelte APIs. - `.tsx` syntax valid. - All JSX tags closed and typed. - Internal links use `to`, external links use `href`. - No broken alias paths (`~/...`). --- ## 12) Critical do/don't summary (hard guardrails) DO - Generate `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx`. - Export a default function component. - Use `AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock` where applicable. - Use only allowed variants. - Preserve placeholders like `{{TOPIC}}`. - Keep content body-only (no layout wrapper). NEVER - Import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Use `bg-white` wrapper backgrounds. - Use unsupported variants (`blue`, `brand` for these DS blocks). - Use `placehold.co`. - Invent imports, components, or props. - Add duplicate page title/breadcrumb header inside content. --- ## 13) Suggested content primitives (repo-aligned) - Section heading: `

...

` - Body copy: `

...

` - Persona block: `AudienceGrid` - Quote/callout: `QuoteBlock` - Image: `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ: `ArticleFAQ` with `ArticleFAQItem[]` - CTA: `ArticleCompanyCTA` - Footer nav: `ArticleFooterNav` --- ## 14) Example scaffold (copy/paste starter) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the first step?', answer: 'Define one clear outcome for {{TOPIC}}.' }, ] export default function ArticleContent() { return (

) } ``` --- ## 15) How to use this prompt 1. Parse inputs (`{{TOPIC}}`, slug, category, sections, FAQ, CTA). 2. org_config_snapshot.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

pillars.name: AI Community Events and Meetups in Australia
pillars.description: This pillar focuses on discovering, choosing, and getting value from AI meetups, workshops, lightning talks, and community events across Australia. It directly supports MLAI's events programming for people who want practical learning, relevant peers, and a clearer entry point into the local AI community.
pillars.content_angle: A practical, community-first guide to finding real AI connections and hands-on learning through Australian events rather than generic networking.

pillars.name: AI Hackathons and Real-World Challenge Programs
pillars.description: This pillar covers hackathons, challenge-based programs, team formation, prototyping, and applied AI problem-solving. It aligns closely with MLAI's hackathons and event programs, helping builders, students, founders, and domain experts turn ideas into tangible projects.
pillars.content_angle: Show how hackathons can be practical pathways to collaboration, prototypes, and startup momentum, not just short-term coding competitions.

pillars.name: Practical AI Learning and Getting Started
pillars.description: This pillar helps beginners and early-stage practitioners learn AI and machine learning through accessible, community-based pathways. It supports MLAI's educational content, workshops, and events by answering common learning questions with practical next steps and applied examples.
pillars.content_angle: Lower the barrier to entry by pairing plain-English education with hands-on community participation instead of theory-heavy content alone.

pillars.name: AI Founder Support, Pitching and Investor Readiness
pillars.description: This pillar addresses how early-stage AI founders can build momentum, sharpen their pitch, create better updates, and start investor relationship-building earlier. It maps to MLAI's founder-oriented tools, practical guides, and community programs that help entrepreneurs move from idea to credible signal.
pillars.content_angle: Focus on founder communication and investor readiness as practical habits supported by community, feedback, and early visibility.
seed_keywords: how to pitch an ai startup

pillars.name: Cross-Disciplinary AI Collaboration
pillars.description: This pillar explores how technical builders, clinicians, researchers, and other domain experts can work together on real-world AI challenges. It reflects MLAI's community and hackathon model, where useful outcomes depend on better collaboration between technical and non-technical participants.
pillars.content_angle: Bridge the gap between builders and domain experts with practical guidance for team formation, problem definition, and applied outcomes.
seed_keywords: technical and non technical collaboration ai

pillars.name: Community Participation and Contributor Recognition
pillars.description: This pillar explains how members can contribute meaningfully inside a community, build credibility, and earn recognition through participation. It directly connects to MLAI's Slack community and Roo Points system, giving contributors a clearer path to involvement, visibility, and useful perks.
pillars.content_angle: Position contribution as a practical way to build relationships and credibility in an AI community, without framing it as a guaranteed pathway to jobs or compensation.
seed_keywords: how to contribute to a tech community

pillar_strategy.rationale: These pillars were chosen because they map directly to MLAI's strongest audience needs and core offerings: events, hackathons, practical learning, founder support, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and community participation. Together they cover the main discovery journeys of learners, builders, founders, contributors, and domain experts while staying grounded in MLAI's role as a practical, community-first home base for applied AI in Australia.

spec.title: What is supervised machine learning for builders?
spec.meta_description: What is supervised machine learning? Learn labels and uses.

summary_items.label: What is meant by supervised machine learning?
summary_items.description: Supervised machine learning trains a model with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. The model learns patterns so it can predict outputs for new data.

summary_items.label: What is supervised and unsupervised learning?
summary_items.description: Supervised learning uses labelled examples with known target outputs. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known output labels.

summary_items.label: Is ChatGPT supervised or unsupervised learning?
summary_items.description: Classify the learning setup rather than the product name alone. AI systems can use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods across different stages.

summary_question_candidates.question: What is meant by supervised machine learning?
What is supervised and unsupervised learning?
Is ChatGPT supervised or unsupervised learning?

faq_items.question: When should a team use supervised learning?
faq_items.answer: Use supervised learning when the target output is clear and you have enough examples with known answers. It is a strong fit for prediction and classification workflows.

faq_items.question: What are labelled examples?
faq_items.answer: Labelled examples pair input data with the correct answer, category, value, or outcome. They give the model a reference point for learning.

faq_items.question: What is the difference between classification and regression?
faq_items.answer: Classification predicts a category or class. Regression predicts a continuous value or quantity. Both are supervised learning tasks when they use labelled examples.

faq_items.question: Why test a supervised model on unseen data?
faq_items.answer: Testing on unseen data checks whether the model has learned useful patterns rather than only remembering training examples. This helps judge whether it can generalise.

faq_items.question: What should builders do before choosing an algorithm?
faq_items.answer: Define the target output and inspect the labels. Clear, consistent labels usually matter more than model complexity at the start.

sections.heading: What is supervised machine learning?
paragraphs: Supervised machine learning is a way to train an AI model using labelled examples. Each example includes an input and a known correct output. The model uses those examples to learn the relationship between the data it receives and the answer it should produce.

The aim is not only to do well on the training examples. The aim is to learn patterns that can generalise to new, unseen data. For builders and founders, supervised learning is most useful when the outcome is clear enough to label, such as a category, prediction, score, or other known target.

sections.heading: How supervised learning works
paragraphs: Supervised learning starts with labelled data. Each example includes an input and the correct output, answer, category, value, or outcome. The labels act as the reference point for learning, because they show the model what the right result should look like.

During training, an algorithm uses those examples to learn relationships between inputs and outputs. The model makes predictions, compares them with the known answers, and adjusts its internal parameters to reduce errors. The aim is not just to remember the training data. The aim is to produce a model that can make useful predictions on new data it has not seen before.

subsections.heading: 1. Labelled examples
paragraphs: A labelled example pairs the data with the correct answer. For a classification task, the label might be a category. This labelled set gives the model its ground truth.

subsections.heading: 2. Training and adjustment
paragraphs: The algorithm processes many labelled examples and looks for patterns. When its prediction is wrong, the model adjusts its parameters.

subsections.heading: 3. Testing on new data
paragraphs: After training, the model is used on new inputs. Instead, it applies the relationships learned from labelled data to predict the likely output.

sections.heading: Classification and regression are the two core tasks
paragraphs: Most supervised machine learning problems fit into one of two task types: classification or regression. Both use labelled examples. Each training example includes an input and the correct output, so the model can learn the relationship between them and make predictions on new data.
Classification is used when the output is a category or class.
This difference matters before you choose an algorithm or tool. Start by naming the target output you want the model to predict.

sections.heading: When builders should choose supervised learning
paragraphs: Choose supervised learning when your project has a clear thing to predict and enough past examples with known answers. The training data needs both inputs and correct outputs, because the model learns the relationship between them. That makes supervised learning a strong fit for prediction and classification work, such as deciding which category a new item belongs to or estimating an outcome from existing data.

For Australian founders, students and AI teams, the practical test is simple: can you write down the target before you train the model? If the answer is yes, and you can collect examples that show that target consistently, supervised learning may be a good starting point. If the answer is no, the project may still be valuable, but it may not be ready for supervised learning yet.

subsections.heading: Start with labels, not model complexity
paragraphs: Good labels matter more than a more complex model at the start. Supervised learning depends on labelled data, where each example includes the correct answer or output. If those answers are noisy, inconsistent or unclear, the model has weak ground truth to learn from.
Before choosing algorithms, teams should check whether people would label the same examples in the same way. This early data work helps make the learning problem clearer.

subsections.heading: Do not force it onto discovery problems
paragraphs: Supervised learning is less suitable when the desired outcomes or categories are not known yet. In those cases, the team is still exploring the structure of the data rather than teaching a model from known answers. That is closer to the role of unsupervised learning, which is commonly contrasted with supervised learning because it does not rely on labelled training examples.

A useful rule is to ask whether you are trying to predict a known answer or discover what the possible answers might be. If you already know the answer type, supervised learning can help automate prediction on new data. If you are still defining the answer type, spend more time on exploration and problem framing before building a supervised model.

sections.heading: A practical path to a first supervised model
paragraphs: Name what the model will receive as input and what it should output. This keeps the work tied to the core idea of supervised learning: labelled examples show the model the relationship between inputs and correct outputs. For example, a task might be to predict a category, a score, or another known result, but the important point is that each training example needs a trusted answer.

Next, build a small labelled dataset and inspect it before training. Then train a simple baseline model and compare its predictions with the known outputs, rather than relying on intuition. The final step is to test the model on examples it did not train on. If it performs well enough on unseen data, the next experiment may justify more data or better features. If it does not, revisit the target, the labels, or the approach before scaling the work.

sections.heading: Common questions about supervised learning
paragraphs: Supervised machine learning means learning from labelled examples. Each training example includes input data and a known output, sometimes called the target or correct answer. The model uses those examples to learn a relationship between the inputs and outputs, then applies that relationship to new data. This is why supervised learning is often described as useful for prediction and classification tasks.

The main difference between supervised and unsupervised learning is the presence of those target labels. Supervised learning starts with examples that show the desired output. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known outputs. Supervised learning is not only classification, either. It is also better to classify the learning setup, not the product name alone. An AI system may use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods.

sections.heading: Turn the definition into a small experiment
paragraphs: Supervised machine learning becomes easier to understand when you turn it into a small test. Start with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. Pick one clear target output, then train a model to learn the relationship between the inputs and that output. It is to make useful predictions on new examples the model has not learned from.

A focused classification or prediction task is usually a better starting point than a broad AI product idea. Before you collect more data or choose a more complex model, discuss the problem with peers, collaborators, or mentors who understand the context.

cta.title: Turn a supervised learning idea into a small test
cta.body: Start with one clear prediction target, a small labelled dataset, and a simple baseline model. Use MLAI resources and community support to pressure-test the problem before scaling.

notes: Grounded sections support the required featured-article shell and explain supervised learning through labelled data, classification, regression, project fit, and first-experiment planning. Summary labels use the first three supplied question candidates verbatim. FAQ items avoid repeating those summary questions.

import { useEffect } from 'react'
import type { ReactNode } from 'react'
import { Home } from 'lucide-react'
import { RocketLaunchIcon, AcademicCapIcon, UsersIcon } from '@heroicons/react/24/outline'
import { DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY, getAuthorProfile, DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL } from '~/articles/authors'
import { ArticleFAQ } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFAQ'
import ArticleCompanyCTA from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA'
import AuthorBio from '../../../components/AuthorBio'
import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader'
import { ArticleImageBlock } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleImageBlock'
import { ArticleFooterNav } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFooterNav'
import QuoteBlock from '../../../components/articles/QuoteBlock'
import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '../../../components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder'
import AudienceGrid from '../../../components/articles/AudienceGrid'
import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA'
import { ArticleStepList } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleStepList'
import MLAITemplateResourceCTA from '../../../components/articles/MLAITemplateResourceCTA'
import { ArticleReferences } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleReferences'
import ArticleDisclaimer from '../../../components/articles/ArticleDisclaimer'

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "What is supervised machine learning for builders?"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "what-is-supervised-machine-learning-for-builders"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-13"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-13"
export const DESCRIPTION = "What is supervised machine learning? Learn labels and uses."
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-80e66436-5e59-491d-8bd3-95efb0595766.jpg?alt=media&token=b966dbd0-be0b-4b7b-8cef-a0c1ab9bc2e0"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "Builder labeling training data on a laptop during a candid supervised machine learning workshop"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "ai"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "When should a team use supervised learning?", answer: "Use supervised learning when the target output is clear and you have enough examples with known answers. It is a strong fit for prediction and classification workflows." },
  { id: 2, question: "What are labelled examples?", answer: "Labelled examples pair input data with the correct answer, category, value, or outcome. They give the model a reference point for learning." },
  { id: 3, question: "What is the difference between classification and regression?", answer: "Classification predicts a category or class. Regression predicts a continuous value or quantity. Both are supervised learning tasks when they use labelled examples." },
  { id: 4, question: "Why test a supervised model on unseen data?", answer: "Testing on unseen data checks whether the model has learned useful patterns rather than only remembering training examples. This helps judge whether it can generalise." },
  { id: 5, question: "What should builders do before choosing an algorithm?", answer: "Define the target output and inspect the labels. Clear, consistent labels usually matter more than model complexity at the start." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: What is supervised machine learning for builders?",
  intro: "What is supervised machine learning? Learn labels and uses.",
  items: [
    { label: "What is meant by supervised machine learning?", description: "Supervised machine learning trains a model with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. The model learns patterns so it can predict outputs for new data." },
    { label: "What is supervised and unsupervised learning?", description: "Supervised learning uses labelled examples with known target outputs. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known output labels." },
    { label: "Is ChatGPT supervised or unsupervised learning?", description: "Classify the learning setup rather than the product name alone. AI systems can use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods across different stages." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "What is supervised machine learning for builders?",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "What is meant by supervised machine learning?", answer: "Supervised machine learning trains a model with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. The model learns patterns so it can predict outputs for new data." },
  { question: "What is supervised and unsupervised learning?", answer: "Supervised learning uses labelled examples with known target outputs. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known output labels." },
  { question: "Is ChatGPT supervised or unsupervised learning?", answer: "Classify the learning setup rather than the product name alone. AI systems can use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods across different stages." },
  { question: "When should a team use supervised learning?", answer: "Use supervised learning when the target output is clear and you have enough examples with known answers. It is a strong fit for prediction and classification workflows." },
  { question: "What are labelled examples?", answer: "Labelled examples pair input data with the correct answer, category, value, or outcome. They give the model a reference point for learning." },
  { question: "What is the difference between classification and regression?", answer: "Classification predicts a category or class. Regression predicts a continuous value or quantity. Both are supervised learning tasks when they use labelled examples." },
  { question: "Why test a supervised model on unseen data?", answer: "Testing on unseen data checks whether the model has learned useful patterns rather than only remembering training examples. This helps judge whether it can generalise." },
  { question: "What should builders do before choosing an algorithm?", answer: "Define the target output and inspect the labels. Clear, consistent labels usually matter more than model complexity at the start." },
]

const faqStructuredData = faqSchemaItems.length
  ? JSON.stringify({
      '@context': 'https://schema.org',
      '@type': 'FAQPage',
      mainEntity: faqSchemaItems.map((item) => ({
        '@type': 'Question',
        name: item.question,
        acceptedAnswer: {
          '@type': 'Answer',
          text: item.answer,
        },
      })),
    })
  : null

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window === 'undefined') return
    if (!new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).has('cfInspector')) return
    const script = document.createElement('script')
    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

        <div id="section-01" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-01"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What is supervised machine learning?"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-01"}>
        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"Supervised machine learning is a way to train an AI model using labelled examples. Each example includes an input and a known correct output. The model uses those examples to learn the relationship between the data it receives and the answer it should produce."}</p>
        <p>{"The aim is not only to do well on the training examples. The aim is to learn patterns that can generalise to new, unseen data. For builders and founders, supervised learning is most useful when the outcome is clear enough to label, such as a category, prediction, score, or other known target."}</p>
        <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-01"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Hero image"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-01"}>

            {

            {

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:key-insight"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Key insight"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Key insight" variant="purple">
            {"Supervised machine learning trains a model with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. The model learns patterns so it can predict outputs for new data."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>
        <div id="section-02" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"How supervised learning works"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <h2>{"How supervised learning works"}</h2>
          <p>{"Supervised learning starts with labelled data. Each example includes an input and the correct output, answer, category, value, or outcome. The labels act as the reference point for learning, because they show the model what the right result should look like."}</p>
          <p>{"During training, an algorithm uses those examples to learn relationships between inputs and outputs. The model makes predictions, compares them with the known answers, and adjusts its internal parameters to reduce errors. The aim is not just to remember the training data. The aim is to produce a model that can make useful predictions on new data it has not seen before."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: How supervised learning works"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-266b2f75-fb2c-44fa-a4ea-75cc4291fae5.jpg?alt=media&token=fd65e095-5a78-4cb7-9777-e3276ddf41ff"
            alt="Notebook with labeled data examples beside a laptop, illustrating supervised learning from inputs and correct outputs"
            caption="How supervised learning works"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <h3>{"1. Labelled examples"}</h3>
          <p>{"A labelled example pairs the data with the correct answer. For a classification task, the label might be a category. This labelled set gives the model its ground truth."}</p>
          <h3>{"2. Training and adjustment"}</h3>
          <p>{"The algorithm processes many labelled examples and looks for patterns. When its prediction is wrong, the model adjusts its parameters."}</p>
          <h3>{"3. Testing on new data"}</h3>
          <p>{"After training, the model is used on new inputs. Instead, it applies the relationships learned from labelled data to predict the likely output."}</p>
        </div>
        <div id="section-03" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Classification and regression are the two core tasks"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <h2>{"Classification and regression are the two core tasks"}</h2>
          <p>{"Most supervised machine learning problems fit into one of two task types: classification or regression. Both use labelled examples. Each training example includes an input and the correct output, so the model can learn the relationship between them and make predictions on new data."}</p>
          <p>{"Classification is used when the output is a category or class."}</p>
          <p>{"This difference matters before you choose an algorithm or tool. Start by naming the target output you want the model to predict."}</p>
        </div>
        <div id="section-04" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-04"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"When builders should choose supervised learning"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-04"}>
          <h2>{"When builders should choose supervised learning"}</h2>
          <p>{"Choose supervised learning when your project has a clear thing to predict and enough past examples with known answers. The training data needs both inputs and correct outputs, because the model learns the relationship between them. That makes supervised learning a strong fit for prediction and classification work, such as deciding which category a new item belongs to or estimating an outcome from existing data."}</p>
          <p>{"For Australian founders, students and AI teams, the practical test is simple: can you write down the target before you train the model? If the answer is yes, and you can collect examples that show that target consistently, supervised learning may be a good starting point. If the answer is no, the project may still be valuable, but it may not be ready for supervised learning yet."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-04"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: When builders should choose supervised learning"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-04"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-1d18a364-072e-4d12-b99e-0aa1494ed1ae.jpg?alt=media&token=8db5a1cd-ec0c-4c9f-a9bf-87c81f292af0"
            alt="Laptop on a cluttered desk showing labeled training data for a supervised learning project"
            caption="When builders should choose supervised learning"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <h3>{"Start with labels, not model complexity"}</h3>
          <p>{"Good labels matter more than a more complex model at the start. Supervised learning depends on labelled data, where each example includes the correct answer or output. If those answers are noisy, inconsistent or unclear, the model has weak ground truth to learn from."}</p>
          <p>{"Before choosing algorithms, teams should check whether people would label the same examples in the same way. This early data work helps make the learning problem clearer."}</p>
          <h3>{"Do not force it onto discovery problems"}</h3>
          <p>{"Supervised learning is less suitable when the desired outcomes or categories are not known yet. In those cases, the team is still exploring the structure of the data rather than teaching a model from known answers. That is closer to the role of unsupervised learning, which is commonly contrasted with supervised learning because it does not rely on labelled training examples."}</p>
          <p>{"A useful rule is to ask whether you are trying to predict a known answer or discover what the possible answers might be. If you already know the answer type, supervised learning can help automate prediction on new data. If you are still defining the answer type, spend more time on exploration and problem framing before building a supervised model."}</p>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Get the checklist"}>

        <div id="section-05" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-05"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"A practical path to a first supervised model"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-05"}>
          <h2>{"A practical path to a first supervised model"}</h2>
          <p>{"Name what the model will receive as input and what it should output. This keeps the work tied to the core idea of supervised learning: labelled examples show the model the relationship between inputs and correct outputs. For example, a task might be to predict a category, a score, or another known result, but the important point is that each training example needs a trusted answer."}</p>
          <p>{"Next, build a small labelled dataset and inspect it before training. Then train a simple baseline model and compare its predictions with the known outputs, rather than relying on intuition. The final step is to test the model on examples it did not train on. If it performs well enough on unseen data, the next experiment may justify more data or better features. If it does not, revisit the target, the labels, or the approach before scaling the work."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-05"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: A practical path to a first supervised model"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-05"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-ddcf890c-9cdd-4797-baef-8f1caa155fb8.jpg?alt=media&token=35ef3629-75f8-44a3-a537-04d396f2bae0"
            alt="Fingertips labeling training data on a laptop for a first supervised machine learning model"
            caption="A practical path to a first supervised model"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>
        <div id="section-06" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-06"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Common questions about supervised learning"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-06"}>
          <h2>{"Common questions about supervised learning"}</h2>
          <p>{"Supervised machine learning means learning from labelled examples. Each training example includes input data and a known output, sometimes called the target or correct answer. The model uses those examples to learn a relationship between the inputs and outputs, then applies that relationship to new data. This is why supervised learning is often described as useful for prediction and classification tasks."}</p>
          <p>{"The main difference between supervised and unsupervised learning is the presence of those target labels. Supervised learning starts with examples that show the desired output. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known outputs. Supervised learning is not only classification, either. It is also better to classify the learning setup, not the product name alone. An AI system may use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods."}</p>
        </div>
        <div id="section-07" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-07"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Turn the definition into a small experiment"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-07"}>
          <h2>{"Turn the definition into a small experiment"}</h2>
          <p>{"Supervised machine learning becomes easier to understand when you turn it into a small test. Start with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. Pick one clear target output, then train a model to learn the relationship between the inputs and that output. It is to make useful predictions on new examples the model has not learned from."}</p>
          <p>{"A focused classification or prediction task is usually a better starting point than a broad AI product idea. Before you collect more data or choose a more complex model, discuss the problem with peers, collaborators, or mentors who understand the context."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-07"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Turn the definition into a small experiment"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-07"}>

        <div className="my-12 not-prose" data-cf-component-id={"cta"} data-cf-component-type={"company-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Company CTA"}>
          <ArticleCompanyCTA
            title="Turn a supervised learning idea into a small test"
            body="Start with one clear prediction target, a small labelled dataset, and a simple baseline model. Use MLAI resources and community support to pressure-test the problem before scaling."
            buttonText="Explore practical AI learning"
            buttonHref="/practical-ai-learning-and-getting-started"
          />
        </div>
      </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"author-bio"} data-cf-component-type={"author-bio"} data-cf-component-label={"About the Author"}>
          <AuthorBio author={authorDetails} />
        </div>

        <div className="mt-12" data-cf-component-id={"faq"} data-cf-component-type={"faq"} data-cf-component-label={"FAQ"}>
          <ArticleFAQ items={faqItems} />
        </div>

        <ArticleFooterNav backHref="/articles" topHref="#" />
    </>
  )
}

import { useEffect } from 'react'
import type { ReactNode } from 'react'
import { Home } from 'lucide-react'
import { RocketLaunchIcon, AcademicCapIcon, UsersIcon } from '@heroicons/react/24/outline'
import { DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY, getAuthorProfile, DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL } from '~/articles/authors'
import { ArticleFAQ } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFAQ'
import ArticleCompanyCTA from '../../../components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA'
import AuthorBio from '../../../components/AuthorBio'
import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader'
import { ArticleImageBlock } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleImageBlock'
import { ArticleFooterNav } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleFooterNav'
import QuoteBlock from '../../../components/articles/QuoteBlock'
import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '../../../components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder'
import AudienceGrid from '../../../components/articles/AudienceGrid'
import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA'
import { ArticleStepList } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleStepList'
import MLAITemplateResourceCTA from '../../../components/articles/MLAITemplateResourceCTA'
import { ArticleReferences } from '../../../components/articles/ArticleReferences'
import ArticleDisclaimer from '../../../components/articles/ArticleDisclaimer'

export const useCustomHeader = true

const TOPIC = "What is supervised machine learning for builders?"
export const CATEGORY = "featured"
export const SLUG = "what-is-supervised-machine-learning-for-builders"
export const DATE_PUBLISHED = "2026-05-13"
export const DATE_MODIFIED = "2026-05-13"
export const DESCRIPTION = "What is supervised machine learning? Learn labels and uses."
const HERO_IMAGE = "https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-80e66436-5e59-491d-8bd3-95efb0595766.jpg?alt=media&token=b966dbd0-be0b-4b7b-8cef-a0c1ab9bc2e0"
const HERO_IMAGE_ALT = "Builder labeling training data on a laptop during a candid supervised machine learning workshop"
export const FEATURED_FOCUS = "ai"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)
const AUTHOR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.name ?? 'Dr Sam Donegan'
const AUTHOR_ROLE = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.role ?? AUTHOR_PROFILE?.credentials ?? 'Founder'
const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {
  id: number
  question: string
  answer: ReactNode
}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "When should a team use supervised learning?", answer: "Use supervised learning when the target output is clear and you have enough examples with known answers. It is a strong fit for prediction and classification workflows." },
  { id: 2, question: "What are labelled examples?", answer: "Labelled examples pair input data with the correct answer, category, value, or outcome. They give the model a reference point for learning." },
  { id: 3, question: "What is the difference between classification and regression?", answer: "Classification predicts a category or class. Regression predicts a continuous value or quantity. Both are supervised learning tasks when they use labelled examples." },
  { id: 4, question: "Why test a supervised model on unseen data?", answer: "Testing on unseen data checks whether the model has learned useful patterns rather than only remembering training examples. This helps judge whether it can generalise." },
  { id: 5, question: "What should builders do before choosing an algorithm?", answer: "Define the target output and inspect the labels. Clear, consistent labels usually matter more than model complexity at the start." },
]

export const summaryHighlights = {
  heading: "Key facts: What is supervised machine learning for builders?",
  intro: "What is supervised machine learning? Learn labels and uses.",
  items: [
    { label: "What is meant by supervised machine learning?", description: "Supervised machine learning trains a model with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. The model learns patterns so it can predict outputs for new data." },
    { label: "What is supervised and unsupervised learning?", description: "Supervised learning uses labelled examples with known target outputs. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known output labels." },
    { label: "Is ChatGPT supervised or unsupervised learning?", description: "Classify the learning setup rather than the product name alone. AI systems can use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods across different stages." },
  ],
}

export const articleMeta = {
  title: "What is supervised machine learning for builders?",
  topic: TOPIC,
  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,
}

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "What is meant by supervised machine learning?", answer: "Supervised machine learning trains a model with labelled examples, where each input has a known correct output. The model learns patterns so it can predict outputs for new data." },
  { question: "What is supervised and unsupervised learning?", answer: "Supervised learning uses labelled examples with known target outputs. Unsupervised learning looks for structure in data without those known output labels." },
  { question: "Is ChatGPT supervised or unsupervised learning?", answer: "Classify the learning setup rather than the product name alone. AI systems can use supervised learning, unsupervised learning, or a mix of methods across different stages." },
  { question: "When should a team use supervised learning?", answer: "Use supervised learning when the target output is clear and you have enough examples with known answers. It is a strong fit for prediction and classification workflows." },
  { question: "What are labelled examples?", answer: "Labelled examples pair input data with the correct answer, category, value, or outcome. They give the model a reference point for learning." },
  { question: "What is the difference between classification and regression?", answer: "Classification predicts a category or class. Regression predicts a continuous value or quantity. Both are supervised learning tasks when they use labelled examples." },
  { question: "Why test a supervised model on unseen data?", answer: "Testing on unseen data checks whether the model has learned useful patterns rather than only remembering training examples. This helps judge whether it can generalise." },
  { question: "What should builders do before choosing an algorithm?", answer: "Define the target output and inspect the labels. Clear, consistent labels usually matter more than model complexity at the start." },
]

const faqStructuredData = faqSchemaItems.length
  ? JSON.stringify({
      '@context': 'https://schema.org',
      '@type': 'FAQPage',
      mainEntity: faqSchemaItems.map((item) => ({
        '@type': 'Question',
        name: item.question,
        acceptedAnswer: {
          '@type': 'Answer',
          text: item.answer,
        },
      })),
    })
  : null

classes=String(node.className||'').toLowerCase();var semantic=String(node.getAttribute('data-semantic')||'').toLowerCase();var aria=String(node.getAttribute('aria-label')||'').toLowerCase();var text=cleanText(node).toLowerCase();if(semantic.indexOf('toc')>=0||aria.indexOf('contents')>=0)return'toc';if(classes.indexOf('reference')>=0||aria.indexOf('reference')>=0||text.indexOf('authoritative
